paragraphs.text: “And God blessed Noah and his sons” (Gen 9:1), that they might grow and multiply, and that the fear of them might be upon all flesh, in the sea and on the dry land. “Only flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat” (Gen 9:4) — that is, do not eat of an animal not slain, nor of meat from which the blood has not yet flowed out, in which is the life of the animal. So God leaves to Noah and his children three covenants: first, He commands them not to eat the blood of animals; second, He promises a resurrection in which blood will be required from all beasts; third, He pronounces that every murderer must be put to death. “For your blood… will I require it of every beast… and at the hand of man” (Gen 9:5). God requires blood both here and in the age to come. Here He requires it, appointing death to the murderer, and therefore He commanded that even an ox which gored a man be stoned (Ex 21:28). And He will require it at the end, because on the day of resurrection the beasts will give back the human flesh they have devoured. “At the hand of man, his brother, will I require the soul” of man, as He appointed punishment to Cain also for the blood of Abel. The same is meant by these words: “Whoso sheddeth man’s blood, in its place shall his blood be shed by men.” And by the words “for in the image of God made he man” (Gen 9:6), it is shown that man, like God, is invested with power to give life and to put to death.

Having concluded the covenant with Noah and all who had come out of the ark with him, God says: “Neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood” (Gen 9:11). And behold, “My bow I do set in the cloud, and it shall be for a sign of the (everlasting) covenant between God… and every flesh that is upon the earth” (Gen 9:13, 17).

After this Moses writes how Noah planted a vineyard, “and drank of the wine, and was drunken, and slept and was uncovered within his tent” (Gen 9:21), and how Ham saw “the nakedness of his father, and told in the midst of the street to his two brothers” (Gen 9:22). Noah’s drunkenness came not from drinking much, but because he had not drunk wine for a long time. In the ark he drank no wine, for on the day of the destruction of all flesh he could not even think to bring wine with him into the ark. So Noah did not taste wine during the year of the Flood; nor did he plant a vineyard in the year he came out of the ark, because he came out on the twenty-seventh day of the month of Iyar, at a time when there could not be even unripe clusters, and it was not possible to plant a vineyard. Therefore not earlier than the third year did Noah plant the vineyard with seeds from dried grapes which he had taken into the ark; and earlier than the third or fourth year afterward he could not have obtained clusters from them. Therefore the righteous man did not taste wine for six years.

Of this long abstinence — and that it was the cause of the drunkenness — testifies also the saying that Ham went out and told his brothers “in the midst of the street.” How could there be streets, if a city had not been built among them? And if there was already a city among them, then no doubt it had been many years in building. So the building of the city and of the street within the city testifies that the elder, as we have said, for many years did not taste wine, and that this was the cause of his drunkenness.

Ham’s brothers knew of their father’s purity, and that he, as Jacob afterward, both waking and sleeping was guarded by an angel; and therefore “covered the nakedness” of him, and that in such wise that with their eyes “they saw not their father’s nakedness” (Gen 9:23).

“And Noah awoke… and knew what his younger son had done unto him” (Gen 9:24). So Noah both slept and was awake: he slept, because he did not feel his nakedness; he was awake, because he knew how his younger son had acted toward him. And Noah cursed him. “And he said: Cursed be Canaan the youth: a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren” (Gen 9:25). Of what would Canaan have been guilty, if he were still in the loins of his father, and the nakedness of Noah was seen not by him but by his father? It is said: Ham received a blessing with all who entered the ark, and then again the blessing was given to him with all who came out of the ark, and so God curses him not in his own person, but in the person of his son, though the cursing of the son must have brought sorrow upon the father too.

Others, on the basis of what Scripture says: “Noah knew what his younger son had done unto him,” since it is known that Ham was not the younger son but the middle one — conclude that by “the younger son” is meant Canaan, and that this little Canaan mocked the nakedness of the elder; while Ham went out with a laughing face and announced it in the midst of the street to his brothers. Therefore it may be thought that, although Canaan was cursed not entirely justly — having done this in childhood — yet not unjustly either, because he was not cursed for another. Moreover, Noah knew that if Canaan in old age would not become worthy of a curse, then even in childhood he would not have done a deed deserving a curse.

Justice required that Ham be deprived of the blessing, not subjected to the curse. For if he had been subjected to the curse for mocking together with the youth, then though he would have been cursed in justice, yet together with cursed Ham all of Ham’s descendants would have been cursed, who had no part at all in the mocking. Therefore Canaan was cursed as the one who mocked, and Ham was only deprived of the blessing because he laughed together with the mocker.

Pronouncing the curse upon Ham in the person of his son, Noah blesses Shem and Japheth and says: “May God enlarge Japheth, and may he dwell in the tents of Shem, and let Canaan the youth be a servant unto him” (Gen 9:27). The race of Japheth multiplied and became mighty in the inheritance to the north and west. God Himself dwelt in the tent of Abraham, the descendant of Shem; and Canaan became a servant of Shem and Japheth, because in the days of Joshua son of Nun the descendants of Shem laid waste the dwellings of the Canaanites, subdued and enslaved their princes.
